essentials.heading: RDS engines, Multi-AZ vs Read Replicas
essentials.body: **RDS** is managed relational DB: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, plus Aurora. **Multi-AZ** = HA: a **synchronous** standby in another AZ, automatic **failover** via DNS CNAME swap (60-120s). Standby is *not* readable — it is for resilience, not scaling. **Read Replicas** = scale reads: **asynchronous** replication (eventual consistency), up to 15 per source, can be **cross-region**, each has its own endpoint, and can be **promoted** to a standalone DB. Mnemonic: Multi-AZ for availability, Read Replicas for performance. You can combine both.

essentials.heading: Aurora
essentials.body: AWS-built MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible engine. **Storage auto-scales** 10 GB to 128 TB. Data is kept as **6 copies across 3 AZs** (4/6 needed for writes, 3/6 for reads), self-healing. Up to **15 Aurora Replicas** share the same storage (low-lag reads, fast failover). **Aurora Global Database**: one primary region + up to 5 read-only secondary regions, <1s replication, for cross-region DR and low-latency global reads. **Serverless v2** auto-scales capacity (ACUs) for variable/unpredictable workloads. Reader/writer endpoints abstract failover.

essentials.heading: DynamoDB
essentials.body: Fully managed **NoSQL** key-value/document DB, single-digit-ms latency, serverless. Items are located by **partition key** (hash) and optional **sort key**. Capacity: **on-demand** (pay-per-request, unpredictable spiky traffic, no planning) vs **provisioned** (set RCU/WCU, cheaper for steady load, supports **auto scaling**). **GSI** = different partition+sort key, queries across whole table, own capacity, eventually consistent, addable anytime. **LSI** = same partition key, alternate sort key, must be created at table creation, shares table capacity. **DAX** = in-memory cache giving microsecond reads. **Streams** = ordered change log (24h) for triggers/replication. **Global Tables** = multi-region active-active. **TTL** = auto-expire items by timestamp attribute.

essentials.heading: ElastiCache: Redis vs Memcached + strategies
essentials.body: Managed in-memory cache for microsecond latency. **Redis**: persistence, replication, **Multi-AZ with automatic failover**, read replicas, backups/snapshots, sorted sets/pub-sub/geospatial, transactions — pick for HA and rich data structures. **Memcached**: pure multi-threaded cache, simple key/value, horizontal sharding, **no persistence, no replication** — pick for a simple, scalable, ephemeral cache. Strategies: **Lazy loading** (cache-aside: read DB on miss, write to cache; risk of stale data) and **Write-through** (write to cache on every DB write; data fresh but more writes). Add **TTL** to bound staleness.

essentials.heading: Redshift (OLAP) vs RDS (OLTP)
essentials.body: **Redshift** is a **columnar, MPP data warehouse** for **OLAP** — analytics, BI, aggregations over huge datasets, complex joins/scans. Loads from S3, scales to petabytes, integrates with QuickSight; **not** for high-throughput transactional writes. **RDS/Aurora** is **row-based** for **OLTP** — many small reads/writes, low-latency single-record transactions (app backends). Rule: 'analyze/report on lots of data' -> Redshift; 'application transactions' -> RDS. Use **Redshift Spectrum** to query S3 directly; **Athena** is the serverless query-S3 alternative.

essentials.heading: Choosing the right database
essentials.body:
- **Relational (RDS/Aurora)**: structured data, joins, ACID transactions, SQL. 
- **Key-value/document (DynamoDB)**: massive scale, flexible schema, predictable single-digit-ms access by key, serverless. 
- **In-memory (ElastiCache)**: caching, session stores, leaderboards, microsecond reads. 
- **Data warehouse (Redshift)**: OLAP analytics over large historical datasets. 
- Others: **DocumentDB** (MongoDB-compatible), **Neptune** (graph), **Timestream** (time-series), **QLDB** (ledger), **Keyspaces** (Cassandra). Match access pattern + scale + consistency needs to the engine.

essentials.heading: Backup, restore, snapshots, PITR
essentials.body: **RDS automated backups**: daily snapshot + transaction logs enable **point-in-time recovery (PITR)** within the retention window (1-35 days); deleted with the instance unless you take a final snapshot. **Manual DB snapshots**: user-triggered, retained until you delete them, shareable/copyable cross-region. Restoring a snapshot always creates a **new** instance/endpoint. **Aurora** uses continuous backups to S3 with PITR and fast clones. **DynamoDB**: **PITR** (restore to any second in last 35 days) plus **on-demand backups**; **Global Tables** add multi-region durability.

labs.title: Create a Multi-AZ RDS PostgreSQL instance
labs.intro: Provision a small **Multi-AZ** PostgreSQL instance to see the synchronous standby and automatic failover HA setup. Multi-AZ is a single endpoint; the standby is hidden and not readable.
labs.cli:
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier saa-lab-pg \
  --db-instance-class db.t3.micro \
  --engine postgres \
  --allocated-storage 20 \
  --master-username saaadmin \
  --master-user-password 'ChangeMe123!' \
  --multi-az \
  --backup-retention-period 7 \
  --no-publicly-accessible

# Watch it come up
aws rds describe-db-instances \
  --db-instance-identifier saa-lab-pg \
  --query 'DBInstances[0].{Status:DBInstanceStatus,MultiAZ:MultiAZ,Endpoint:Endpoint.Address}'
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

db = aws.rds.Instance(
    "saa-lab-pg",
    identifier="saa-lab-pg",
    engine="postgres",
    instance_class="db.t3.micro",
    allocated_storage=20,
    username="saaadmin",
    password="ChangeMe123!",  # use pulumi.Config secret in real code
    multi_az=True,
    backup_retention_period=7,
    publicly_accessible=False,
    skip_final_snapshot=True,
)

export_endpoint = db.endpoint
labs.note: Cleanup: `aws rds delete-db-instance --db-instance-identifier saa-lab-pg --skip-final-snapshot --delete-automated-backups`. Multi-AZ roughly doubles cost (standby runs continuously). Trigger a test failover with `aws rds reboot-db-instance --db-instance-identifier saa-lab-pg --force-failover`.

labs.title: Create a DynamoDB table (on-demand) with a GSI
labs.intro: Create a serverless table using **on-demand** (PAY_PER_REQUEST) capacity and add a **Global Secondary Index** to query by a non-key attribute. On-demand removes capacity planning; the GSI enables a second access pattern.
labs.cli:
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name saa-lab-orders \
  --attribute-definitions \
      AttributeName=orderId,AttributeType=S \
      AttributeName=customerId,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=orderId,KeyType=HASH \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST \
  --global-secondary-indexes '[{
      "IndexName": "by-customer",
      "KeySchema": [{"AttributeName":"customerId","KeyType":"HASH"}],
      "Projection": {"ProjectionType":"ALL"}
  }]'

# Query the GSI
aws dynamodb query --table-name saa-lab-orders \
  --index-name by-customer \
  --key-condition-expression 'customerId = :c' \
  --expression-attribute-values '{":c":{"S":"cust-1"}}'
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

orders = aws.dynamodb.Table(
    "saa-lab-orders",
    name="saa-lab-orders",
    billing_mode="PAY_PER_REQUEST",
    hash_key="orderId",
    attributes=[
        aws.dynamodb.TableAttributeArgs(name="orderId", type="S"),
        aws.dynamodb.TableAttributeArgs(name="customerId", type="S"),
    ],
    global_secondary_indexes=[
        aws.dynamodb.TableGlobalSecondaryIndexArgs(
            name="by-customer",
            hash_key="customerId",
            projection_type="ALL",
        )
    ],
    ttl=aws.dynamodb.TableTtlArgs(attribute_name="expiresAt", enabled=True),
)
labs.note: Cleanup: `aws dynamodb delete-table --table-name saa-lab-orders`. With PAY_PER_REQUEST a GSI inherits on-demand billing (no separate RCU/WCU to set). Remember: GSIs can be added anytime; **LSIs must be defined at table creation**.

questions.stem: A company runs a production RDS MySQL database. They need automatic failover to another Availability Zone if the primary fails, with no data loss. Which configuration meets this?
options: Create five Read Replicas in the same region
Add a cross-region Read Replica and promote it on failure
questions.explanation: Multi-AZ keeps a synchronous standby in another AZ and fails over automatically with no data loss — exactly the HA requirement. Read Replicas are asynchronous (potential data loss) and require manual promotion, so the cross-region replica and five replicas options are about read scaling/DR, not automatic same-failover HA. Hourly snapshots provide backups, not automatic failover.

questions.stem: An application's RDS PostgreSQL primary is CPU-bound by heavy reporting SELECT queries that slow down user transactions. The team wants to offload the read load. What should they do?
options: Convert the instance to Multi-AZ so the standby serves reads
Create one or more Read Replicas and point reporting queries at them
questions.explanation: Read Replicas have their own endpoints and are made to offload read traffic — reporting queries hit the replica, freeing the primary. A Multi-AZ standby cannot serve reads (it exists only for failover). Backup retention and PITR are data-protection features and do nothing for read scaling.

questions.stem: A gaming app has highly unpredictable, spiky traffic and needs single-digit-millisecond key-value access at any scale with no capacity planning. Which database and capacity mode fit best?
options: DynamoDB with provisioned capacity and no auto scaling
questions.explanation: DynamoDB on-demand handles spiky, unpredictable traffic with no capacity planning and gives single-digit-ms key-value access — the ideal match. Provisioned capacity without auto scaling risks throttling on spikes. RDS is relational/OLTP and not a no-planning key-value store at this scale. Redshift is an OLAP warehouse, wrong workload entirely.

questions.stem: A read-heavy web app repeatedly queries the same product data from RDS, causing high DB load and latency. The team wants microsecond reads and high availability with automatic failover for the cache. Which solution? (Choose two)
options: ElastiCache for Redis with Multi-AZ enabled
questions.explanation: Redis supports replication and Multi-AZ with automatic failover, giving a highly available cache; pairing it with a lazy-loading (cache-aside) strategy caches data on first miss to cut repeated DB reads. Memcached has no replication or failover, so it fails the HA requirement. Moving to Redshift is wrong — Redshift is for OLAP analytics, not a transactional app cache.

questions.stem: A retailer needs to run complex analytical queries and aggregations over years of historical sales data for BI dashboards. Which AWS database is purpose-built for this?
questions.explanation: Redshift is a columnar MPP data warehouse built for OLAP — large-scale aggregations and BI analytics over historical data. DynamoDB is key-value (no complex analytical joins/scans at warehouse scale). RDS is OLTP/row-based and not optimized for petabyte analytical scans. ElastiCache is an in-memory cache, not an analytics store.

questions.stem: A company wants a relational database that auto-scales storage, keeps six copies of data across three AZs for durability, and can serve low-latency reads from multiple regions for a global user base. Which option?
options: RDS MySQL Multi-AZ in a single region
questions.explanation: Aurora stores 6 copies across 3 AZs with auto-scaling storage, and Aurora Global Database adds up to 5 read-only secondary regions for low-latency global reads and DR. RDS Multi-AZ is single-region and the standby isn't readable. DynamoDB Global Tables fit the requirement but DynamoDB is NoSQL, not relational. Redshift is an analytics warehouse, not an OLTP relational DB.

questions.stem: A DynamoDB table is queried mostly by partition key, but a new feature must efficiently query items by an attribute that is not part of the table's key. The table is already in production. What should be added?
questions.explanation: A GSI defines a different partition (and optional sort) key and can be added to an existing table at any time, enabling queries on the new attribute. An LSI must be created at table creation and only provides an alternate sort key under the same partition key, so it cannot be added now nor change the partition key. Streams capture changes (not a query index); DAX is a read cache that doesn't add a new access pattern.

questions.stem: An administrator accidentally deleted critical rows in an RDS database 20 minutes ago. Automated backups are enabled with a 7-day retention. What is the best recovery method?
options: Restore the oldest manual snapshot available
Restore using point-in-time recovery to just before the deletion
questions.explanation: With automated backups, point-in-time recovery can restore to any second within the retention window (here just before the deletion), minimizing data loss. Promoting a Read Replica copies the same deletion (async replicated). A failover reboot doesn't undo the data change. The oldest manual snapshot would lose far more recent data than PITR.
